caveats: a: No six-digit OEWS occupation table is published for 313220.
a: The product mix spans commodity tape and elastic through engineered webbing and decorative embroidery, so task exposure varies.
a: Existing computer-controlled production is part of the baseline and is not credited as AI exposure.
rho: The manufacturing AI evidence is economy-wide and includes establishments much larger than the target band.
rho: Some exposed office tasks may be easier to implement than production-facing inspection or optimization.

e: Firm and establishment boundaries can differ materially for multi-plant textile groups.
e: Schiffli embroidery and engineered narrow fabrics can have different customer and asset profiles.

s5: Owner age creates succession pressure but does not establish transaction probability or transferability.

q: BLS announced discontinuation of selected 313220 PPIs, limiting ongoing price visibility.
q: Bargaining power varies between commodity ribbon or tape and highly qualified webbing.

d5: Pandemic-era comparisons distort the 2021 starting point.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

identity.title: Narrow Fabric Mills and Schiffli Machine Embroidery

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Repeat B2B manufacture sold by specification, lot, length, production run, or contract order, often with customer-specific material, performance, design, or finishing requirements.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive internal production, shell entities, non-control financing vehicles, firms outside the normalized EBITDA band, and businesses without repeat external-customer orders.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market mills repeatedly supplying external customers with woven or braided narrow fabrics, elastic narrow fabrics, labels, ribbons, tape, webbing, covered rubber thread, and Schiffli machine embroidery.

a.bridge: BLS occupation data measure broader NAICS 3132 Fabric Mills, not wage-weighted task exposure in 313220. Fabric Mills employed 8,310 textile knitting and weaving machine operators and 4,280 winding, twisting, and drawing-out operators in May 2024, indicating a heavily physical workforce. I infer that AI exposure is concentrated in specification intake, design-file handling, quoting, scheduling, purchasing, quality analytics, maintenance triage, sales service, and administration. The broader population may misstate 313220's mix in either direction; counting conventional machinery or digitized embroidery as new AI would bias exposure upward, so it is excluded.
caveats: No six-digit OEWS occupation table is published for 313220.
The product mix spans commodity tape and elastic through engineered webbing and decorative embroidery, so task exposure varies.
Existing computer-controlled production is part of the baseline and is not credited as AI exposure.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted task inventory from at least ten 313220 mills, separating currently automated loom or embroidery-machine work from paid hours in design preparation, quoting, scheduling, inspection, maintenance, procurement, and administration.
a.rationale: Narrow-fabric and embroidery production combines machine operation, setup, material handling, and physical quality control with a smaller layer of information-heavy customization and coordination. AI can reduce parts of the latter but cannot replace most plant-floor execution within five years.

d5.bridge: BLS real sectoral output for the broader 3132 Fabric Mills population fell from 88.543 in 2021 to 77.156 in 2024, with the decline narrowing from 2023 to 2024; Federal Reserve industrial production was nearly flat from 74.6653 in 2023 to 75.8120 in 2025. I use that structural decline plus recent stabilization to bound 313220 demand. Broader 3132 includes broadwoven, nonwoven, and knit mills, so its mix may understate resilient engineered narrow-fabric demand or overstate it if niche 313220 products lose share; hours-based industrial-production detail may bias downward when productivity rises.
caveats: Both output series are 3132 rather than 313220.
The NAICS combines product categories with materially different end markets and substitution risks.
Pandemic-era comparisons distort the 2021 starting point.
d5.change_evidence: USITC import and domestic-shipment quantities by narrow-fabric HTS and product class, plus five-year customer program volumes and backlog for a representative mix of elastic, label, tape, webbing, braid, and embroidery mills.
d5.rationale: Labels, ribbons, tape, elastic, webbing, braids, covered thread, and embroidery remain physical inputs across multiple end markets, but imports, offshore apparel production, substitution, customer consolidation, and lower domestic textile output constrain growth. Recent broader-fabric stabilization supports a base case of moderate decline rather than continued steep contraction.

caveats: The estimate is applied to frozen n=56 and does not re-estimate that dataset input.
Firm and establishment boundaries can differ materially for multi-plant textile groups.
Schiffli embroidery and engineered narrow fabrics can have different customer and asset profiles.
e.change_evidence: A 313220 firm-level census linking establishments to ultimate owners, normalized EBITDA, captive status, external-customer revenue share, customer concentration, and repeat-order percentage.
e.rationale: The enumerated products are normally manufactured for external buyers in recurring industrial or apparel supply relationships, making a substantial share conceptually eligible. The frozen count can nevertheless include captive plants, divisions of larger firms, marginal or distressed mills, pure commodity producers without durable relationships, and businesses whose normalized EBITDA or control status fails diligence.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 313220 ($291,231,552) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 313220 ($978,937,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1525 (margins.json[31]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Food Processing EBITDA/Sales 15.25% (dominant subsector of NAICS 31)); revenue/employee $189,240 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 313220); class <5: 54 firms, avg revenue/firm $391,185 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $59,656 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 31 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,472,194 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $224,510 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 27 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,009,185 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $458,901 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 35 firms, avg revenue/firm $7,218,886 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,100,880 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 18 firms, avg revenue/firm $22,178,222 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $3,382,179 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 3 firms, avg revenue/firm $59,686,000 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $9,102,115 -> IN $1-10M band; n_band = 56

caveats: Operator requirement is a bounded judgment, not a directly measured share.
Some decorative embroidery or label demand could be eliminated digitally or shifted into printed substitutes; that effect overlaps with d5 and is not double counted.
o.change_evidence: Buyer interviews and program-level substitution studies quantifying volume expected to move to customer self-manufacture, imports of finished downstream goods, printed or molded substitutes, or production technologies that bypass a narrow-fabric or embroidery operator.
o.rationale: Customers ultimately require physical narrow fabrics, thread, webbing, labels, ribbons, or embroidery. Digital design, automated ordering, and AI scheduling can reduce coordination labor but cannot supply the material product or remove responsibility for equipment, material traceability, specification compliance, inspection, and delivery.

caveats: PPI measures output price, not retention of a gross AI benefit.
BLS announced discontinuation of selected 313220 PPIs, limiting ongoing price visibility.
Bargaining power varies between commodity ribbon or tape and highly qualified webbing.
q.change_evidence: Invoice and contract cohorts around implemented automation, split among fixed-price, indexed, toll-conversion, and competitive-bid arrangements, with renewal pricing, contribution margin, win/loss, and customer-sharing clauses.
q.rationale: Order- and specification-based product pricing can preserve early savings, especially where qualification, custom patterns, short runs, and quality performance matter. Retention declines through buyer rebids, import competition, contractual indexing, and competitor adoption. BLS reported the 313220 PPI at 254.869 in May 2025 and 255.961 in June 2025, while September 2025 percent-change entries were largely unavailable, so price evidence is too thin to treat pass-through as observed.

caveats: The manufacturing AI evidence is economy-wide and includes establishments much larger than the target band.
Some exposed office tasks may be easier to implement than production-facing inspection or optimization.
rho.change_evidence: Implementation cohorts from narrow-fabric MES, ERP, computer-vision, embroidery-digitization, and predictive-maintenance vendors showing project completion, integration cost, defect outcomes, hours saved, and sustained use after two years.
rho.rationale: Standardized office automation, order/specification extraction, scheduling support, and analytics should be implementable, but mill-specific machines, fragmented design and ERP data, small technical teams, customer qualification, and costly quality errors constrain deployment. Census manufacturing research found any AI at 22.8% of establishments but production using AI at only 8.0%, while cost was cited as a barrier by 43.2% and lack of expertise by 12.3%.

s5.bridge: Census 2021 ABS research reports 62.4% of women owners of manufacturing employer businesses were age 55 or older in data year 2020, a broad manufacturing and women-owned population rather than 313220. I convert that succession pressure to a 3%-7% annual qualifying-control-transfer hazard and compound across five years, then discount for family transfers, closures, and non-operating asset sales. The population difference and absence of male-owned firms can bias either way; using owner age as if it directly measured sales would bias upward.
caveats: The proxy covers women-owned manufacturing employer firms, not 313220 specifically.
Owner age creates succession pressure but does not establish transaction probability or transferability.
s5.change_evidence: Five years of PitchBook, GF Data, Axial, BizBuySell, and textile-equipment auction records matched to a 313220 owner roster, with owner age and exclusions for closures, family/internal reorganizations, minority stakes, and asset-only liquidations.
s5.rationale: Specialized, capital-intensive mills may have aging ownership and valuable customer qualifications, but buyer scarcity, equipment condition, environmental or safety liabilities, family succession, and weak growth can delay or prevent a qualifying control transfer.

principal_driver: Repeat specification-based physical products create recurring relationships and information workflows around a still-essential operator.
principal_weakness: The labor base is predominantly physical, and six-digit evidence on AI adoption, demand, and qualifying transfers is sparse.
